page.title: Lina — Co-parenting, simplified | App for two homes
page.description: Lina helps co-parents coordinate everyday life across two homes — care schedule, messages, child information, and shared album in one app.

hero.title: One shared place for your child — across homes
hero.description: You should not have to be the one who always remembers. Lina gives both parents the same overview — medical info, contacts, schedules, and belongings — always up to date, in both homes.

csSection.label: Care Schedule
csSection.title: Plan your care schedule together
csSection.description: Lina's interactive care schedule lets both parents plan and visualize how time is shared across weeks. See day splits, weekend patterns, and handover frequency — all in one clear view.

how.label: How it works
how.title: Set it up once. The rest is shared automatically.

step1.title: Create a shared space
step1.description: One parent sets up a space for the child. The other gets an invite to join. No complicated setup required.

step2.title: Add what matters
step2.description: Medical information, allergies, contacts, equipment lists — add it once, and it is available in both homes.

step3.title: No one needs to ask twice
step3.description: Updates are visible to both parents in real time. No more repeating yourself, or wondering if the other home has the latest.

features.label: Features
features.title: All the details, shared with the other home

threads.title: Threads
threads.description: Separate conversations for each topic — logistics, school, health. Nothing gets buried in one long chat.

messages.title: Messages
messages.description: Messages cannot be edited or deleted. Search conversations, mark what matters, and always have a clear record of what was agreed.

album.title: Shared album
album.description: A private album where both parents can share photos. The moments that happen in one home are visible in both.

features.more: Also included: contacts, equipment lists, child information, and care schedule — so both parents have what they need, wherever the child is.

contacts.title: Shared contacts
contacts.description: Pediatrician, teacher, after-school program — important contacts in one place, always up to date.

equipment.title: Equipment lists
equipment.description: Rain boots, gym bag, allergy medicine — shared lists make it easier to keep track of what needs to follow the child between homes.

childinfo.title: Child information
childinfo.description: Birth date, allergies, medication, shoe size — all the details that matter, gathered in one place and always accessible.

schedule.title: Care schedule
schedule.description: Create a visual overview of how time is shared between homes. See the full schedule at a glance, week by week.

control.title: Built for difficult days too
control.description: Export all messages for documentation. Blur photos to reduce emotional weight. Save the images you want to keep. Control what you see and when.

problem.label: Why Lina
problem.title: When care is split across two homes, details get lost
problem.description: When plans, messages and photos live in different apps, important things fall through the cracks. Lina brings everything into one shared structure — calm, clear and predictable.

forWhom.forDescription: Parents who share care across two homes and want less friction in everyday cooperation. For those who value structure over noise and clarity over constant messaging.
forWhom.notDescription: Not a social network, not a general family chat, and not built for endless conversation. Lina is a focused tool designed specifically for cooperation around your child.

privacy.label: Privacy
privacy.title: Private by design
privacy.description: What happens in Lina stays between the two of you. Your child's information is private — and no one outside has access to what you share.

point1.title: No advertising
point1.description: Your experience is never shaped by ads or commercial interests.

point2.title: No data sharing
point2.description: Your data stays yours. It is never shared with third parties.

point3.title: No algorithm
point3.description: You decide what you see — not an algorithm optimizing for engagement.

privacy.tagline: Structured for cooperation. Private by default.
privacy.pageTitle: Privacy Policy — Lina | GDPR-compliant co-parenting app
privacy.pageDesc: Lina is built privacy-first for co-parenting families. GDPR-compliant, no ads, and no data sharing with third parties. Learn how we protect you.

cta.title: One safe line between two parents.
cta.description: Everything both parents need to know — in one place. Download and get started in minutes.

pricing.pageTitle: Pricing — Lina co-parenting app for two homes
pricing.pageDesc: Lina has a free plan and Lina+ at €7.99/month. One subscription covers both parents. No ads, no data sharing.
pricing.label: Pricing

pricing.description: Lina+ unlocks all features with a single subscription — covering both homes. Start with a free trial.

free.name: Free

pricing.whyText1: Lina is funded by the subscription — not by advertising or data sharing. That is what makes it possible to keep the app independent and focused on what matters.
pricing.whyText2: The subscribing parent automatically gives the other full access. No separate payment, no extra account.

p1.title: One subscription, two parents

p2.title: No advertising, no data sharing

cs.pageTitle: Care Schedule — plan shared custody with Lina
cs.pageDesc: Plan and visualize your child's care schedule between two homes. Free tool with day splits, weekend overview, and PDF export.
cs.heading: Care Schedule
cs.subhead: See how time is divided between homes — week by week.
cs.intro: Click on the days to assign them to a parent. The split updates automatically, and you can export the plan as a PDF.

familievern.pageDesc: Lina helps co-parents coordinate everyday life across two homes. Information about the app for family counselors and professionals.

hero.label: For professionals
hero.title: A tool you can recommend to parents who share care
hero.description: Lina helps parents keep the practical details of everyday co-parenting organized and accessible across two homes. This page is intended for professionals.

about.label: About the app
about.title: One shared place for the child — across homes
about.description: Many parents who share care lack a common structure for everyday cooperation. Information is scattered across texts, emails, and verbal agreements, and one parent ends up coordinating everything. Lina brings the practical details — medical information, contacts, equipment lists, photos, and messages — into one shared space accessible in both homes.

when.label: When Lina is a good fit

fits.title: Lina works well when parents
fits.item1: have a fundamentally workable co-parenting relationship but struggle with the practical coordination
fits.item2: want more structure and predictability in their everyday routines
fits.item3: need a shared communication channel that keeps cooperation focused
fits.item4: want to ensure important information about the child is available in both homes

notFits.title: Lina is not suitable when
notFits.item1: there is high conflict requiring legal or therapeutic intervention
notFits.item2: one or both parents are not ready for structured digital communication
notFits.item3: the situation requires measures beyond practical coordination

privacy.label: Privacy
privacy.title: Secure and private by design
privacy.description: Lina is built with privacy as its foundation. Parents' data is handled responsibly, and the app is free from commercial interests. As a professional, you can recommend it with confidence.

point1.title: No advertising
point1.description: No ads or commercial interests affect the user experience.

point2.title: No data sharing
point2.description: Parents' data is not sold or shared with third parties.

point3.title: Secure infrastructure
point3.description: Information is protected through secure storage, access control, and signed links.

point4.title: GDPR-compliant
point4.description: Lina is fully compliant with the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). You can read the full privacy policy at getlina.app/privacy.

features.label: How it works in practice
features.title: Structure that reduces friction in everyday life

threads.title: Threads
threads.description: Parents communicate about one topic at a time — logistics, school, or health — without important information getting lost in a long message thread.

messages.title: Messages
messages.description: Messages cannot be edited or deleted. This creates a predictable communication channel and a clear record of what has been agreed.

album.title: Shared album
album.description: Both parents can share photos from everyday life. The child is visible in both homes.

info.title: Child information
info.description: Medical information, allergies, and important details are entered once and available to both parents — regardless of which home the child is in.

equipment.title: Equipment lists
equipment.description: A clear overview of what the child needs in each home reduces the number of clarifications and misunderstandings around practical details.

schedule.title: Care schedule
schedule.description: The care arrangement is shown in one shared view. Fewer discussions about who had the child and when.

agreement.title: Care Agreement
agreement.description: The care agreement page gives parents an overview of what the agreement should include, with an example and links to official resources. A good starting point before or after mediation.

thoughtful.label: Thoughtful details
thoughtful.title: Designed with care for sensitive situations

blurPhotos.title: Blur photos
blurPhotos.description: Parents can blur individual photos in the shared album. This helps manage emotional reactions and keeps the focus on practical cooperation.

blurMessages.title: Blur messages
blurMessages.description: Parents can blur message content in a thread. The message is still there, but can be revealed when the reader is ready — reducing pressure in difficult conversations.

markImportant.title: Mark important
markImportant.description: Important messages can be marked and stay easy to find. Agreements, schedules, and key decisions are always accessible — not buried in a long thread.

export.title: Export conversations
export.description: All messages can be exported as a file. If a conversation needs to be shared with a mediator, solicitor, or other professional, parents have a clear record.

csPromo.label: Tools
csPromo.title: A care schedule parents can use together
csPromo.description: Lina includes an interactive care schedule that shows how time is divided week by week. The tool can be used by parents on their own, or as a starting point in conversations about care arrangements.

recommend.label: Recommend Lina
recommend.title: Something to hand to parents
recommend.description: If you'd like to recommend Lina to parents you work with, you can download a one-page overview to print or share digitally. It explains what Lina is, what it includes, and how to get started — in plain language.

contact.label: Contact

contact.description: Do you have questions about Lina, or would you like to discuss whether the app might suit families you work with? We are happy to help.

ca.pageTitle: Care Agreement — what should it include? | Lina
ca.pageDesc: Learn what a good care agreement should cover. See an example, get tips for the process, and find links to official resources.
ca.label: Parenting Agreement
ca.heroTitle: What should a parenting agreement include?
ca.heroDesc: A parenting agreement helps parents create predictability for the child when care is shared across two homes. Here you will find an overview of what the agreement should cover, an example, and links to official resources.
ca.aboutTitle: An agreement that grows with the child
ca.aboutDesc: A parenting agreement is a voluntary, written arrangement between parents describing how care for the child is organised across two homes. It is not legally binding in the same way as a court order, but gives the family a shared framework to follow. A good agreement reflects the child's everyday life — and should be adjusted as the child grows.
ca.point1Title: Not legally binding like a court order
ca.point1Desc: The agreement is private and based on mutual consent between the parents. If you cannot agree, a family mediation service can help.
ca.point2Title: The child's best interests at the centre
ca.point2Desc: The agreement should be based on the child's needs, not the parents'. Children who are old enough should be heard and given the opportunity to express their views.
ca.point3Desc: A verbal agreement is hard to follow up on and easy to misunderstand. A written agreement provides clarity for both parents and can be used as a reference in case of disagreement.
ca.point4Desc: A child's needs change with age. A good parenting agreement is reviewed and adjusted regularly — ideally once a year or when circumstances change significantly.
ca.infoNote: This page provides general information and does not replace legal advice. If you cannot agree, we recommend contacting your local family services.
ca.coverTitle: Points a good parenting agreement should cover
ca.coverIntro: Not all points are relevant for everyone. Include what suits your situation.
ca.cover1Desc: How are the weeks divided? A consistent pattern — for example alternating weeks or a 3-4-4-3 arrangement — gives the child a clear rhythm.
ca.cover2Desc: Does the child have a primary residence with one parent, or shared residence? The residence affects rights and responsibilities.
ca.cover3Desc: Agree on the time and place for handovers. Clear arrangements reduce misunderstandings and give the child a calm transition.
ca.cover4Desc: The allocation of Christmas, Easter, summer holiday, the child's birthday, and other days off should be made explicit.
ca.cover5Desc: How expenses for activities, clothing, equipment, and other needs are shared. Clear agreements prevent disputes.
ca.cover6Desc: How do parents communicate about the child day to day? Agree on the channel, response time, and what is important to report.
ca.cover7Desc: Arrangements for travel abroad: notice period, consent, and where passports and other important documents are kept.
ca.cover8Desc: How are changes to the agreement handled? Agree on a regular review frequency — ideally once a year.
ca.exampleTitle: What a parenting agreement can look like
ca.exampleIntro: Below you can see an example of how a completed parenting agreement might look. Use this as inspiration when creating your own.
ca.tmplMeta: James Smith and Sarah Smith · Created 15 March 2026
ca.tmplResidence: Shared residence. Both parents have equal day-to-day decision-making authority.
ca.tmplSchedule: Alternating weeks, from Monday morning to the following Monday morning. The arrangement takes effect 1 September 2026.
ca.tmplHandover: Handovers take place via nursery or school. The parent beginning their week drops Emma off in the morning and assumes parental responsibility from that point. On days when nursery/school is closed, handover takes place at 4:00 pm at the home of the parent whose week has ended.
ca.tmplXmasDesc: The Christmas holiday is divided into two periods: period 1 (23–27 December) and period 2 (28 December – 1 January). Parents alternate between periods each year.
ca.tmplXmasA: Parent A has period 1 in even-numbered years and period 2 in odd-numbered years.
ca.tmplXmasB: Parent B has period 1 in odd-numbered years and period 2 in even-numbered years.
ca.tmplXmasHandover: Handover between periods takes place at 12:00 noon.
ca.tmplXmasDeadline: Allocation is confirmed in writing by 1 November.
ca.tmplEasterDesc: The Easter holiday runs from the Wednesday before Good Friday through to Easter Monday. Parents alternate each year.
ca.tmplEasterAlt: Parent A has even-numbered years, Parent B has odd-numbered years.
ca.tmplEasterDeadline: Allocation is confirmed in writing by 1 February.
ca.tmplSummerDesc: Each parent may have up to three consecutive weeks of holiday with Emma. Who chooses first alternates each year.
ca.tmplSummerAlt: Parent A chooses first in odd-numbered years, Parent B in even-numbered years.
ca.tmplSummerDeadline: Allocation is confirmed in writing by 1 April.
ca.tmplNationalDesc: Spent with each parent on alternating years.
ca.tmplNationalAlt: Parent A has odd-numbered years, Parent B has even-numbered years.
ca.tmplOtherHolDesc: For public holidays not specifically regulated, the regular arrangement applies. If such a day falls on a handover day, the handover moves to 12:00 noon unless otherwise agreed in writing.
ca.tmplBirthdayDesc: Emma's birthday is celebrated with each parent on alternating years. The hosting parent is responsible for organising and covering costs. Close family from both sides may be invited.
ca.tmplBirthdayAlt: Parent A organises in even-numbered years, Parent B in odd-numbered years.
ca.tmplContactDesc: While Emma is with one parent, she may have phone or video contact with the other 1–2 times per week. The timing is adapted to Emma's routines and mood. Calls are kept brief and calm.
ca.tmplSwapDesc: Changes may be made when both parents agree and it is practically feasible. Predictability is prioritised — frequent swaps are avoided. If there is disagreement, the regular arrangement applies.
ca.tmplComm: Parents communicate in writing about practical matters relating to Emma. The tone should be factual and respectful. Neither parent should speak negatively about the other in Emma's presence.
ca.tmplTravel: Foreign travel with Emma must be notified in writing at least four weeks in advance. Passports are kept with the parent at whose address Emma is registered.
ca.tmplAddressDesc: Emma is registered at Parent A's address. Changing the registered address requires written consent from both parents. In case of disagreement, both parties commit to mediation before any change is made. School placement is discussed no later than one year before school start.
ca.tmplHealthDesc: Both parents must be kept informed of appointments and assessments relating to Emma's health, development, and follow-up from a doctor, health visitor, or nursery/school. Both have the right to attend consultations and meetings.
ca.tmplFamilyDesc: Emma should have the opportunity to build secure relationships with both parents' families and close networks. Both parents should actively facilitate this.
ca.tmplFinanceDesc: Ongoing expenses for Emma are covered from a shared account to which both parents contribute an agreed monthly amount. Child benefit is transferred to the same account. Larger or unexpected expenses are agreed in advance. Both parents have full access to the account.
ca.tmplChangesDesc: This agreement replaces all previous verbal and written agreements between the parents. Changes are only valid when made in writing and signed by both parties. The agreement is reviewed at least every six months to ensure it continues to meet Emma's needs.
ca.tmplDisclaimer: This is an example. Adapt the content to your own situation.
ca.promoDesc: Once the parenting agreement is in place, everyday life begins. Lina helps parents with the rest: messages, child information, photos, and equipment lists — all in one shared place across two homes.
ca.csPromoTitle: Plan the schedule week by week
ca.csPromoDesc: Once the agreement is in place, Lina's interactive care schedule helps you plan and visualize day-to-day life. See day splits, weekend patterns, and handover frequency — all in one clear view.

faq.pageTitle: Frequently Asked Questions — Lina co-parenting app
faq.pageDesc: Answers to common questions about Lina — the co-parenting app. Find answers about privacy, account, care schedule, and care agreement.
faq.heroDesc: Find answers to the most common questions about Lina, the care schedule, and the care agreement.
faq.cat1a1: Lina brings the practical side of co-parenting into one app — messages, child information, photos, contacts, equipment lists, and a care schedule — accessible to both homes.
faq.cat1a2: Lina is made for parents who share care of a child between two homes. The app works best when the basic co-parenting relationship is functional, but more structure and predictability are needed.
faq.cat1a3: Yes, Lina is free to download and use. No payment is required to create an account or invite the other parent.
faq.cat1a4: Lina is available as an app for iPhone and Android. The care schedule at getlina.app can be used in a browser without downloading the app.
faq.cat1q5: Do both parents need to use the app?
faq.cat1a5: To use shared features like messages, photos, and child information, both parents need an account. The care schedule can be used by one parent alone.
faq.cat2q1: What is the care schedule in Lina?
faq.cat2a1: The care schedule is a free tool you can use directly in your browser. Click on days to assign them to each parent and see the summary update instantly. The plan can be exported as a PDF.
faq.cat2q2: Do I need an account to use the care schedule?
faq.cat2a2: No, the care schedule can be used without an account directly in a browser. You only need to download the app to use shared features like messages and child information.
faq.cat2q3: Can I export the care schedule?
faq.cat2a3: Yes, you can export the care schedule as a PDF directly from the tool. The PDF includes a visual overview of the split and can be used as an attachment to a care agreement.
faq.cat2q4: Can I plan holidays and special periods in the care schedule?
faq.cat2a4: Yes, the care schedule supports flexible week-by-week planning, making it easy to add holiday weeks and special periods.
faq.cat3q1: Who can see the child's information?
faq.cat3a1: Only the two parents connected to the child in Lina have access to the child's information. No other users or Lina itself has access to the content you share.
faq.cat3q2: Does Lina share data with third parties?
faq.cat3a2: No. Lina does not sell or share user data with third parties. There is no advertising in the app.
faq.cat3q3: Can messages be deleted or edited?
faq.cat3a3: No, messages in Lina cannot be edited or deleted after they are sent. This provides a clear and reliable record of what has been agreed between parents.
faq.cat3a4: You own your own data. Lina processes data only to deliver the service, and never for commercial purposes.
faq.cat3q5: What happens to my data if I delete my account?
faq.cat3a5: When the account is deleted, all personal information associated with you is removed. Messages, photos, and lists shared with the other parent remain available to them.
faq.cat4q1: How do I create an account?
faq.cat4a1: Download Lina from the App Store or Google Play, and follow the in-app guide to create an account. You only need your email address to sign in — no password is stored.
faq.cat4q2: Can I have multiple children in the app?
faq.cat4a2: Yes, you can add multiple children to Lina. Each child has their own shared space with associated information, messages, and photos.
faq.cat4q3: How do I invite the other parent?
faq.cat4a3: You share the invitation code via the app.
faq.cat4q4: What if the other parent doesn't want to use the app?
faq.cat4a4: Lina only works when both parents are connected. If the other parent is hesitant, it can help to explain what Lina does in practical terms: it's a shared place for information about the child — contacts, medical details, belongings, and messages — so that neither parent has to carry everything alone. It's free to join, takes two minutes to set up, and does not require a credit card. You can share the invite link and let them look at it in their own time.
faq.cat5q1: What should a care agreement include?
faq.cat5a1: A good care agreement should cover the care pattern, place of residence, pick-up and drop-off, holiday and public holiday arrangements, cost sharing, communication between parents, travel with the child, and routines for changes and review of the agreement.
faq.cat5q2: Is a care agreement legally binding?
faq.cat5a2: A private care agreement is based on mutual agreement between the parents. It is not legally binding in the same way as a court order, but provides structure and predictability for both parties and the child.
faq.cat5q3: Is there a free tool for making a care agreement?
faq.cat5a3: In Norway, Bufdir offers a free digital parenting agreement where parents fill out the agreement step by step and sign with BankID. Similar official tools exist in Sweden, Denmark, and Finland.
faq.cat5q4: Does Lina help with care agreements?
faq.cat5a4: Lina offers an information page about what a care agreement should include, with an example and links to official resources in Norway, Sweden, Denmark, and Finland.

about.pageTitle: About Lina — The co-parenting app built for two homes
about.pageDesc: Lina was built for co-parents who want a calm, structured tool for everyday coordination. Designed and built in Norway.
about.heading: About Lina
about.lead: Lina is a co-parenting app for parents who share care of a child across two homes. The same overview, the same information — regardless of which home the child is in.
about.whyText1: Lina was created because there was no co-parenting app that felt calm and focused. Most tools were built around conflict or legal processes. Lina is something different — a quiet, structured space for everyday coordination between two parents.
about.whyText2: The goal is to reduce the mental load that comes with shared care. When both parents have access to the same schedules, lists, and updates, no one has to carry everything alone.
about.focusText1: Lina is built around structured communication and practical tools. Threads keep conversations organized. Shared lists, contacts, and information about the child are always available — visible across both homes.
about.focusText2: There are no algorithms, no ads, and no social features. Lina is designed to be used when needed and to stay out of the way when it is not.

research.pageTitle: Research on co-parenting and child wellbeing — Lina
research.pageDesc: A growing collection of peer-reviewed research on what helps children when parents share care across two homes, summarised in plain language.
research.label: Research
research.heading: Research on co-parenting and child wellbeing
research.lead: A growing collection of peer-reviewed studies on what helps children when parents share care across two homes. Each entry is paraphrased in plain language, with a link back to the original publication, which is always the authoritative source.

findings.title: What the research consistently finds
findings.p1: Across the studies summarised here, no single living arrangement emerges as universally better for children than another. Outcomes vary with many factors, and the arrangement itself is only one of them.
findings.p2: The literature points again and again to the quality of relationships — between the two parents, and between each parent and the child — as at least as important as how time is divided. Parental communication and the level of conflict between parents are recurring variables associated with how children experience the situation.
findings.p3: Children's own experiences of the same arrangement can differ significantly. Family preconditions, such as each parent's health and financial situation, also shape outcomes. Research tends to describe a picture that is specific to each family, rather than one with a single correct answer.

featured.title: Featured study
featured.studyTitle: Samværs- og bostedsordninger etter samlivsbrudd: betydninger for barn og unge
featured.studyTitleEn: Custody and living arrangements after parents separate: implications for children and adolescents
featured.authorsValue: Trine Bjerke Johansen, Heid Nøkleby, Lars Jørun Langøien, Tiril C. Borge
featured.institutionValue: Norwegian Institute of Public Health (Folkehelseinstituttet / FHI), commissioned by the Norwegian Directorate for Children, Youth and Family Affairs (Bufdir)
featured.scopeValue: 40 Nordic studies synthesised across three research questions (consequences, experiences, and factors)
featured.summaryP1: FHI examined how different custody and contact arrangements — including joint physical custody and various contact schedules — relate to children's wellbeing, mental health, family relationships, and parental cooperation. Drawing on 40 Nordic studies, the review could not conclude that one arrangement is consistently better for children than another.
featured.summaryP2: Both qualitative and quantitative studies pointed to the significance of relationships within the family, between the two parents, and between each parent and the child. Parental communication and the level of conflict between parents were recurring factors associated with how children fared. Family preconditions, such as parental health and finances, also influenced outcomes.
featured.summaryP3: The report notes that individual children can experience the same arrangement very differently, and suggests that services meeting separating families work from each family's specifics rather than recommend one arrangement as a default.
featured.linkText: Read the original report at FHI

about.title: About these summaries
about.p1: We paraphrase each study rather than reproduce the original text. Every entry links to the original publication, which is the authoritative source if the summary and the original differ.
about.p2: We are a product team summarising published work to help parents and professionals find it, not the authors of the research. If you spot anything that misrepresents a study, corrections are welcome.

research.closing: More studies will be added over time, including Swedish, Danish, Finnish, and German parallels to the Nordic literature summarised here.

stories.pageTitle: How parents use Lina — co-parenting stories
stories.pageDesc: Small, everyday situations where Lina makes the difference. See how parents use the app to share information, photos, and equipment lists across homes.
stories.label: Everyday scenarios
stories.title: How parents use Lina
stories.description: Small, everyday moments where having one shared place makes the difference. These are the situations Lina is built for.

s1.label: Child information
s1.title: The allergy the other home did not know about
s1.situation: A child is diagnosed with a new food allergy at a doctor's visit. The information needs to reach the other home before the next handover.
s1.resolution: With Lina: The parent updates the child's medical information once. The other parent sees it immediately — no need for a separate text or call.

s2.label: Equipment lists
s2.title: The rain jacket that is always at the wrong house
s2.situation: A child arrives at school without rain gear because no one was sure which home had it. Small things like this create unnecessary friction.
s2.resolution: With Lina: Shared equipment lists show what is at each home. The list stays up to date, so the jacket follows the child.

s3.label: Threads
s3.title: The school event buried in a text chain
s3.situation: School sends information about a parents' meeting. One parent forwards it by text, but the details get lost between practical messages about pickup and dinner.
s3.resolution: With Lina: A dedicated thread for school keeps everything together. Details are easy to find without scrolling through unrelated messages.

s4.label: Shared album
s4.title: The first bicycle ride you almost missed
s4.situation: A child learns to ride a bicycle at one home. The other parent only hears about it days later and never sees the moment.
s4.resolution: With Lina: Photos go into the shared album. Moments are shared automatically — regardless of which home the child is in.

s5.label: Care schedule
s5.title: The handover mix-up on a holiday week
s5.situation: Holiday weeks change the regular schedule. Both parents remember a different version of the plan, and the child ends up in the middle.
s5.resolution: With Lina: The care schedule shows both parents the same overview. Changes are visible immediately, so there is one version of the truth.

s6.label: Contacts
s6.title: The dentist number no one could find
s6.situation: A child has a toothache at one home. The parent needs to call the dentist but does not have the number — it was saved in the other parent's phone.
s6.resolution: With Lina: Shared contacts mean both parents have the dentist, doctor, and school numbers available at all times.

stories.closing: These are not dramatic problems. They are the quiet, everyday moments where a shared overview saves time, reduces stress, and keeps the focus on the child.

termsSummary.heading: Terms summary
termsSummary.disclaimer: This summary is provided for convenience only. In the event of any inconsistency, the English version of the Terms of Service above shall prevail.
termsSummary.p1: Lina is a private cooperation tool for two parents who share responsibility for a child.
termsSummary.p2: You must be at least 18 years old and a parent or legal guardian to use the service. Each Lina space is intended for two connected parents.
termsSummary.p3: You keep ownership of all content you share in Lina. Lina only processes content to provide the service and does not sell or publicly share it.
termsSummary.p4: Lina is offered as a paid subscription. Subscriptions renew automatically unless cancelled through your platform account settings.
termsSummary.p5: Lina is a support tool and does not provide legal, medical, or professional advice, nor does it mediate disputes between parents.
termsSummary.p6: Shared content may remain available to the other parent after account deletion.
termsSummary.p7: For full details, see the Terms of Service.

privacySummary.heading: Privacy summary
privacySummary.disclaimer: This summary is provided for convenience only. In the event of any inconsistency, the English version of the Privacy Policy above shall prevail.
privacySummary.p1: Lina is built with privacy as a core principle.
privacySummary.p2: We process only personal data that is necessary to provide the service and support cooperation between parents. We do not use advertising, tracking, or marketing cookies.
privacySummary.p3: Only strictly necessary local storage is used to remember essential settings, such as Care Schedule preferences. The website uses Cloudflare Web Analytics for anonymous page view statistics — no cookies, no personal data, no cross-site tracking.
privacySummary.p4: When an account is deleted, personal account data is removed. Shared content may remain available to the other parent, as it may be jointly used within the service.
privacySummary.p5: All child-related data is provided and managed by parents or legal guardians.
privacySummary.p6: No personal data is sold or shared for commercial purposes.
privacySummary.p7: For full details, please refer to this Privacy Policy.

howTo.title: How to delete your account
howTo.intro: Users can request deletion of their Lina account in one of the following ways.
howTo.emailBefore: If you cannot access the app, contact us at:
howTo.emailAfter: — please include the email address associated with your Lina account.

whatHappens.title: What happens when you delete your account
whatHappens.item1: Your personal account information (such as email address and profile data) is removed
whatHappens.item2: Your access to Lina is permanently disabled
whatHappens.sharedContent: Shared content (such as messages, photos, and lists) is not automatically deleted, as it may be shared with another parent. This data remains available to the other connected parent.
whatHappens.retention: Some information may be retained temporarily where required by law or for security purposes, after which it is deleted or anonymised.

storiesTeaser.label: Everyday moments
storiesTeaser.title: Built for the moments that matter

s1.title: The allergy the other home did not know about
s1.description: One parent updates the child's medical information. The other sees it immediately — no separate text needed.

s2.title: The rain jacket that is always at the wrong house
s2.description: Shared equipment lists show what is at each home. Updates are visible right away, so the jacket follows the child.

s3.title: The first bicycle ride you almost missed
s3.description: Photos go into the shared album. The big and small moments are shared automatically — regardless of which home the child is in.

app.description: Lina helps co-parents coordinate care across two homes — shared schedules, messages, and lists in one calm, private space.

indexFaq.a: Lina is a co-parenting app that helps parents who share care across two homes. It brings together messages, schedules, medical information, contacts, equipment lists, and photos in one shared, private space.

Lina has a free plan that includes up to 2 threads, 30 photos, messages, child information, contacts, equipment lists, and a care schedule. Lina+ costs €7.99/month and unlocks unlimited threads, a larger album, thread search, thread export, and important messages. One subscription covers both parents.

indexFaq.q: Do both parents need to pay?
indexFaq.a: No. One Lina+ subscription covers both parents. The parent who subscribes gives both parents full access — the other parent never needs to pay.
Yes. Lina has no advertising, no data sharing with third parties, and no algorithm deciding what you see. Your information stays between the two parents.

indexFaq.q: Can messages be edited or deleted?
indexFaq.a: No. Messages in Lina cannot be edited or deleted after sending. This creates a clear, predictable record of what has been communicated and agreed.

faqFaq.a: Lina is a co-parenting app that brings together practical details — child information, messages, photos, contacts, and equipment lists — in one shared place accessible from both homes.
Yes, Lina is free to download and use. No payment is required to create an account or invite the other parent.

faqFaq.q: Can I export the care schedule?
faqFaq.a: Yes, you can export the care schedule as a PDF directly from the tool.

faqFaq.q: Who can see the child's information?
faqFaq.a: Only the two parents linked to the child in Lina have access to the child's information.

faqFaq.q: Can messages be deleted or edited?
faqFaq.a: No, messages in Lina cannot be edited or deleted after sending, creating a clear and reliable record.

csFaq.a: A care schedule is a visual overview of how a child's time is divided between two homes, week by week.
No, the care schedule tool can be used directly in the browser without an account.
Yes, you can export the care schedule as a PDF.

caFaq.q: What should a care agreement include?
caFaq.a: A good care agreement should cover care patterns, residence, pickup and delivery, holiday distribution, cost sharing, communication, travel, and routines for changes.

caFaq.q: Is a care agreement legally binding?
caFaq.a: A private care agreement is based on mutual consent between parents. It is not legally binding like a court order, but provides structure and predictability.

caFaq.q: Where can I create a care agreement?
caFaq.a: In many countries, government family services or mediation centres can help you create a care agreement. Check your local family law authority for digital or in-person options.

pricingFaq.a: Lina has a free plan with basic features. Lina+ costs €7.99 per month with a 2-week free trial.

pricingFaq.q: Does the other parent also need to pay?
pricingFaq.a: No. One Lina+ subscription covers both parents.
Yes. No commitment period. Cancel anytime through the App Store or Google Play.

pricingFaq.q: What is included in the free plan?
pricingFaq.a: Up to 2 threads, 30 photos, messages, child information, contacts, equipment lists, and a care schedule.

blog1.pageDesc: An overview of the most common care arrangements for children with two homes — week on/week off, 3-4-4-3, 2-2-5-5 and other models. What works for different age groups?
blog1.label: Shared care
blog1.title: Care arrangements explained
blog1.intro: When parents share care of a child, the care arrangement is the first thing to settle. How is time split between homes? Which pattern gives children the most predictability? Here is an overview of the most common models — what they involve, and what may suit different situations.
blog1.s1p1: The child lives one week with each parent, switching on a fixed day — usually Monday or Friday. This is the most widely used arrangement when parents share custody equally.
blog1.s1p2: The benefit is a simple structure and predictability. The child always knows which home they are heading to. The downside is that a full week can feel like a long time away from one parent, especially for younger children.
blog1.s1p3: Works best for: School-age children who can manage longer stretches away from one home. Requires both homes to be reasonably close to school and activities.
blog1.s2p1: The child is with one parent for 3 days, the other for 4, then 4 and 3 in the following week. The pattern repeats over two weeks and gives an even 50/50 split.
blog1.s2p2: This arrangement gives more frequent contact with both parents than week on/week off. The child is never away from either parent for more than 4 days in a row. The trade-off is more handovers — which can be demanding if cooperation is difficult.
blog1.s2p3: Works best for: Primary school-age children who need frequent contact with both parents. Requires both parents to live close to each other.
blog1.s3p1: The child is with parent A on Monday and Tuesday, parent B on Wednesday and Thursday, and alternates weekends (Friday to Sunday) with each parent. The result is a 50/50 split over two weeks.
blog1.s3p2: The benefit is that the child has fixed days with each parent during the week, giving a predictable weekly rhythm. The downside is that weekend residence alternates, and there are many transitions per week.
blog1.s3p3: Works best for: Young children who need frequent contact with both parents but also benefit from fixed weekday routines.
blog1.s4title: Every other weekend plus one weekday
blog1.s4p1: The child lives mainly with one parent and stays with the other every other weekend (usually Friday to Sunday) and one fixed weekday. The split is typically 70/30 or 60/40.
blog1.s4p2: This arrangement suits situations where the child has a primary home — for example because the other parent lives far away, or because the child's daily life is closely tied to one neighbourhood or school.
blog1.s4p3: Works best for: Situations where equal sharing is not practically achievable. Can work well for children of all ages, as long as the non-resident parent has meaningful contact during the week.
blog1.s5title: What works for different age groups
blog1.s5p1: The child's age is an important factor when choosing an arrangement. Young children (0–3) often need shorter periods and more frequent transitions — which may mean a 2-2-5-5 or a tailored arrangement works better than week on/week off.
blog1.s5p2: Preschool-age children (3–6) can usually manage 3–4 days away from one parent. From school age (6+), week on/week off works well for many families, as children have more independence and can handle longer absences.
blog1.s5p3: Teenagers (12+) should have a say in the arrangement. Many young people prefer longer stretches in each home rather than frequent transitions, as it gives them more stability in their social lives.
blog1.s6title: When should the arrangement be reviewed
blog1.s6p1: A care arrangement is not set in stone. A child's needs change with age, and an arrangement that worked well at four may not suit a ten-year-old.
blog1.s6p2: It is worth reviewing the arrangement at least once a year — or when significant changes occur in the family's situation, such as a new school, a move, or a new sibling.
blog1.s6p3: The most important thing is that the child experiences predictability, that both parents are available, and that practical details follow the child between homes.
blog1.ctaDesc: Lina's care schedule lets you click in the days and see how time is divided week by week — with a summary of the day split, weekend patterns, and number of transitions.

blog2.pageTitle: Handover day — how to make transitions easier | Lina
blog2.pageDesc: Practical tips for smoother handovers between homes — fixed routines, neutral locations, and what should follow the child.
blog2.label: Handover
blog2.title: Handover day — how to make transitions easier
blog2.intro: For many children, handover day is the hardest part of having two homes. Not because something is wrong, but because the transition requires adjustment. Routines, surroundings, and rules change — and the child has to adapt each time. Here are practical tips for making handovers safer and more predictable.
blog2.s1p1: Predictability is the most important thing for the child. When the handover happens at the same time and place every time, the child does not have to wonder what will happen.
blog2.s1p2: Common times are after daycare or school — the transition happens naturally, without the child experiencing a direct handover between parents.
blog2.s1p3: If a direct handover is necessary, agree on a neutral location and a fixed time. Avoid changing the time at the last minute — it creates uncertainty.
blog2.s2title: Keep the handover short and calm
blog2.s2p1: Long handovers give the child time to feel unsettled. A short, friendly exchange is better than a long one with extended conversation between parents.
blog2.s2p2: Avoid discussing practical or sensitive topics at the handover. The child senses tension between parents, even when the conversation seems neutral.
blog2.s2p3: A simple 'have a good time' and a smile is often enough. Let the child settle into the new home without carrying the parents' communication.
blog2.s3title: What the child needs to know in advance
blog2.s3p1: Children who know what to expect handle transitions better. Tell the child in advance what the plan is: who is picking up, when it happens, and what comes next.
blog2.s3p2: Avoid saying 'we will see' or 'maybe.' Children need clear answers, even when they do not ask directly.
blog2.s3p3: For younger children, a visual calendar — with colours for each home — can help them understand the pattern and feel in control of their week.
blog2.s4p1: One of the most common sources of frustration is something missing at the wrong home. School bag, rain gear, comfort toy, medication — everything the child needs daily should follow them at each handover.
blog2.s4p2: Create a shared packing list that both parents can access. Go through it before each handover and update it as needs change.
blog2.s4p3: Some families solve it by doubling up on essentials — toothbrush, charger, indoor shoes. Others prefer one list that follows the child. Choose what creates the least friction.
blog2.s5title: When the handover is not working
blog2.s5p1: Sometimes the child cries, refuses to go, or is visibly unsettled. This does not necessarily mean something is wrong with the arrangement — transitions are hard, and children show it in different ways.
blog2.s5p2: If the pattern continues, talk to the other parent about what can be adjusted. Perhaps the timing is wrong, the child needs more preparation, or the arrangement should be reviewed.
blog2.s5p3: If difficulties persist, a family counsellor or mediation service can help find solutions that fit the child's needs.
blog2.s6p1: The child adjusts better when both parents are aligned. This does not mean agreeing on everything — but that the child experiences both parents supporting the arrangement.
blog2.s6p2: Share practical information in advance: what the child has been doing, anything to be aware of, whether something is missing from the bag. Brief and factual, not controlling.
blog2.s6p3: A shared overview of equipment, medical information, and contacts reduces the number of questions at each handover — and lets the child stop being the messenger between homes.
blog2.ctaDesc: Lina brings together equipment lists, child information, and contacts in one shared place — so both parents have what they need at every handover.

blog3.pageTitle: What your child needs in each home — Lina
blog3.pageDesc: A practical checklist for co-parents — what should be in both homes, what should follow the child, and how to keep track.
blog3.label: Equipment
blog3.title: What your child needs in each home
blog3.intro: When a child has two homes, the same question comes up again and again: is the toothbrush here or there? Where is the rain gear? Do we have enough nappies? A well-thought-out overview of what should be where saves time, reduces frustration, and gives the child a more settled everyday life.
blog3.s1p1: Some things should be available in both homes, so the child does not have to think about packing the essentials. The idea is that the child can arrive at each home and have what they need — without a big bag.
blog3.s1p2: Typical basics: toothbrush and toothpaste, hairbrush, their own bedding, towels, a pair of indoor shoes, enough clothes for the stay (including underwear and socks), and a charger for phone or tablet if applicable.
blog3.s1p3: For younger children: nappies, dummy, drinking bottle, a favourite book or soft toy (if the child has several), and childproofing where needed.
blog3.s2title: Things that should follow the child
blog3.s2p1: Anything that exists in only one copy — and the child needs daily — should come along at every handover. This typically includes the school bag, lunch kit, specific clothes for activities, and medical equipment.
blog3.s2p2: Medication and allergy equipment are especially important. Both parents should know which medications the child uses, the dosage, and where the equipment is. Ideally it should be in both homes — but if not, it must always follow the child.
blog3.s2p3: The favourite stuffed animal or comfort blanket is easy to forget — but can make a big difference to the child's sense of safety, especially at bedtime in the other home.
blog3.s3p1: Needs change with the seasons. Snowsuit, mittens, and hat in winter. Swimwear and sunscreen in summer. Rain gear and wellies in spring and autumn.
blog3.s3p2: Not everything needs to be doubled — but it helps to review what is needed in each home at the change of season. A quick check in September and March can save many mornings without the right winter jacket.
blog3.s3p3: Write down what is in each home and update the list when things move or get replaced.
blog3.s4p1: Needs change as the child grows. A small child needs nappies and a dummy. A school-age child needs a backpack, gym kit, and perhaps an instrument. A teenager needs a charger, keys, and bus money.
blog3.s4p2: Let the child take part in the process when old enough. Children from about seven or eight can have their own small checklist to use before each handover.
blog3.s4p3: Teenagers should largely manage this themselves, but it still helps for parents to have a shared overview — especially for medical equipment, documents, and things that are expensive to replace.
blog3.s5title: Avoid making the child the messenger
blog3.s5p1: A common pattern is the child becoming the one who remembers and carries information between homes. 'Tell dad that…' or 'remember to bring…' places responsibility on the child that really belongs to the parents.
blog3.s5p2: A shared equipment list that both parents can see and update removes this burden from the child. The parents check the list — the child does not have to remember everything.
blog3.s5p3: The same goes for messages. Practical information should go directly between parents, not through the child.
blog3.s6p1: The key is having one shared place where both parents can see what is in each home. Whether it is an app, a spreadsheet, or a simple list on the fridge — what matters is that both have access.
blog3.s6p2: Update the list regularly. Clothes that are too small, broken equipment, and seasonal changes should be caught before they become a problem on a busy morning.
blog3.s6p3: Treat the list as a practical tool — not as control. The goal is that the child has what they need, regardless of which home they are in.
blog3.ctaTitle: Shared equipment lists for both homes
blog3.ctaDesc: Lina's shared equipment lists let both parents see what is in each home — and update in real time. So the equipment follows the child.

blog4.pageTitle: Shared residence vs. primary residence — what is the difference? | Lina
blog4.pageDesc: The difference between shared residence and primary residence with one parent — what it means in practice for everyday life, decision-making, and the child's rights.
blog4.label: Residence
blog4.title: Shared residence vs. primary residence — what is the difference?
blog4.intro: When parents separate, one of the first questions is where the child will live. The answer is not just about the number of nights — it affects who decides what, where the child is registered, and how everyday life is organised. Here is an overview of what shared residence and primary residence actually mean.
blog4.s1p1: Shared residence means the child lives equally with both parents. Both have equal decision-making authority on matters affecting the child's daily life — such as activities, medical appointments, and everyday routines.
blog4.s1p2: In practice, this usually means an approximately 50/50 split of time, for example alternating weeks or a 3-4-4-3 pattern. The child has two homes, not one home and a visiting address.
blog4.s1p3: Shared residence requires that the parents cooperate well enough to make ongoing decisions together. It also requires that the homes are close enough for the child to maintain school, friends, and activities from both places.
blog4.s2p1: Primary residence means the child has one main home. The resident parent has decision-making authority on daily matters and the child is registered at that address.
blog4.s2p2: The other parent — the contact parent — has contact according to an agreement, for example every other weekend and one weekday. The contact parent still shares parental responsibility, but daily decisions are made by the resident parent.
blog4.s2p3: This arrangement is often used when the parents live far apart, when the child needs one stable base, or when cooperation between the parents is difficult.
blog4.s3p1: With shared residence, both parents have equal decision-making authority on daily matters. Neither parent can move with the child without the other's consent.
blog4.s3p2: With primary residence, the resident parent can make daily decisions alone. The resident parent may in principle move domestically with the child, but must notify the other parent.
blog4.s3p3: Joint parental responsibility is maintained regardless of the residence arrangement — unless a court decides otherwise. Parental responsibility covers major decisions such as passports, medical treatment, and religion.
blog4.s4p1: With shared residence, the parents share the daily tasks: clothes, equipment, homework, medical appointments. Both homes need to be fully equipped, and both parents must be available for everyday life.
blog4.s4p2: With primary residence, everyday life is more predictable for the child — one neighbourhood, one set of routines, one school route. But it requires extra effort to ensure the contact parent stays involved in the child's life.
blog4.s4p3: Neither arrangement is objectively better than the other. What works depends on the child's age, the parents' situation, the distance between homes, and the quality of cooperation.
blog4.s5p1: Research from Scandinavia shows that children with shared residence generally report equal or better wellbeing compared to children living primarily with one parent — provided the level of conflict between parents is low.
blog4.s5p2: For children in high-conflict situations, the picture is more complex. Frequent transitions between two homes can increase stress if the parents are unable to shield the child from the conflict.
blog4.s5p3: Regardless of the arrangement, it is the quality of parental cooperation — not the number of nights — that matters most for the child's wellbeing.
blog4.s6p1: The choice of residence should be made with the child's best interests at heart. If you cannot agree, family mediation services can help.
blog4.s6p2: In Norway, Bufdir provides information on parental cooperation. In Sweden, the municipal family law office (familjerätten) can assist. In Denmark, it is Familieretshuset, and in Finland the municipal child welfare officer (lastenvalvoja).
blog4.s6p3: This article provides general information and does not replace legal advice. If in doubt, contact a professional.
blog4.ctaTitle: Coordinate everyday life — regardless of arrangement
blog4.ctaDesc: Whether the child has shared residence or lives primarily with one parent — Lina helps both parents keep track of the practical details.

blog5.pageDesc: How to keep communication factual and structured when sharing care of a child — practical tips on threads, written agreements, and clear boundaries.
blog5.label: Communication
blog5.title: Communication between co-parents
blog5.intro: For most co-parents, communication is the hardest part. Not the big decisions — but the daily messages. Who is picking up? What happened at the doctor? Does the child need new rain gear? When communication works, everyday life works. When it does not, the child notices first.
blog5.s1p1: Messages between parents should be about the child, not about the relationship between the parents. This is easier said than done — especially in the time right after a separation.
blog5.s1p2: A simple rule: write the message as if a neutral third party were reading it. Would you be comfortable with a counsellor or mediator seeing what you wrote? If yes, send it. If not, rephrase.
blog5.s1p3: This does not mean everything has to be clinical. Friendly and factual can coexist. A simple 'thanks for sorting that out' costs nothing and builds cooperation over time.
blog5.s2p1: Verbal agreements are hard to remember precisely. After a few weeks, the parents remember different versions of what was said — and the child ends up in the middle.
blog5.s2p2: Written communication gives both parents a reference to go back to. It reduces misunderstandings and makes it easier to follow up on agreements.
blog5.s2p3: It does not need to be formal. A short message with the essentials — time, place, what is needed — is enough. The point is that it is documented.
blog5.s3p1: One long message thread where school information, medical appointments, holiday planning, and everyday logistics are mixed together is a recipe for things getting lost.
blog5.s3p2: Separate threads for different topics — school, health, logistics — make it easier to find what you are looking for. You do not have to scroll through twenty messages about dinner to find the doctor's appointment.
blog5.s3p3: It also gives more control. You can deal with the health thread in the evening and the logistics thread in the morning — without everything piling up in one chaotic stream.
blog5.s4p1: Not responding is also communication. If a parent sends a message about pickup and gets no reply, it creates uncertainty — for the parent and for the child.
blog5.s4p2: Agree on a reasonable response time for practical messages. Not the same minute, but within a few hours for urgent things, and within a day for what can wait.
blog5.s4p3: If you do not have an answer yet, say so: 'I will check and get back to you tonight.' That removes the uncertainty without requiring an immediate decision.
blog5.s5p1: Sometimes communication stops working. One parent stops responding, the tone escalates, or everything becomes a battle. This is common — and help is available.
blog5.s5p2: Family counselling services offer mediation and guided conversations for parents struggling with cooperation. In some cases, a neutral third party can help restore a functioning communication channel.
blog5.s5p3: If direct communication is too difficult, a structured tool with written messaging and a permanent message history can help. Messages that cannot be edited or deleted give both parents confidence that what was agreed is actually documented.
blog5.s6title: The child should not be the messenger
blog5.s6p1: One of the most common mistakes is using the child as a communication channel. 'Tell mum that…' or 'ask dad about…' puts the child in an impossible position.
blog5.s6p2: The child should not have to relay messages, negotiate agreements, or report back from the other home. All practical communication should go directly between the parents.
blog5.s6p3: If the child shares something from the other home, listen — but avoid asking probing questions. The child needs to feel they can speak freely without becoming an information source.
blog5.ctaDesc: Lina keeps conversations organised in separate threads. Messages cannot be edited or deleted — so both parents have a clear record of what was agreed.

blog6.pageTitle: Summer holidays across two homes | Lina
blog6.pageDesc: Practical guidance for co-parents on planning summer holidays — how to split the break, handle travel and consent, and keep the child settled across two homes.
blog6.label: Holidays
blog6.title: Summer holidays across two homes
blog6.intro: Summer is the longest stretch of the year when everyday routines stop. For co-parents sharing care, that means longer blocks away from one home, travel plans across borders, and decisions that need to hold up months in advance. The first summer after a separation is often the hardest — but the patterns you set now carry into every summer after.
blog6.s1p1: Daily and weekly arrangements are built around school, work, and nearby routines. Summer breaks most of that — schools close for several weeks, both parents usually take vacation, grandparents travel, and summer camps fill up early.
blog6.s1p2: The result is that the regular care schedule rarely carries through July and August without changes. Most families agree on a separate summer arrangement each year, documented in advance.
blog6.s1p3: Treat the summer plan as its own agreement, not a variation of the weekly schedule. It needs its own dates, its own handovers, and its own written version that both parents can point to.
blog6.s2p1: By the time one parent asks about summer in late May, it is often too late. Flight prices have risen, summer camps are full, and grandparents have booked their own plans. The other parent's vacation weeks may already be locked in at work.
blog6.s2p2: A reasonable rule: have the main decisions made by April. That means deciding which weeks each parent will have the child, whether travel abroad is planned, and whether any camps or courses will be booked.
blog6.s2p3: This does not mean every day needs to be scheduled in April. Flexibility is fine — but the large blocks should be agreed early. Small adjustments in June are much easier than building the whole plan from scratch at that point.
blog6.s3p1: Halving the summer is the simplest — each parent has half the break in one continuous block. It works well when both parents want longer trips and when the child is old enough for a longer stretch away from each home. Alternating two-week blocks is often better for younger children, and for parents who live close enough that contact during each block is easy.
blog6.s3p2: Alternating full summers is used by parents who live far apart — different countries, or cities with long travel times. One parent has the full summer one year, the other the next. The parent without summer care typically gets a larger share of another holiday to balance it.
blog6.s3p3: For public holidays that fall inside summer — midsummer, national days, bank holidays — many families alternate year by year. Parent A has midsummer in odd years, Parent B in even years. Write it down, so the question does not come up again.
blog6.s4p1: If either parent plans to travel abroad with the child, written consent from the other parent is usually required — by airlines, by border authorities, and in some cases by the destination country. Requirements vary, so check what the specific countries involved ask for.
blog6.s4p2: Know who has the child's passport, and check that it is valid for the trip — some countries require six months of validity beyond the return date. A short signed letter from the non-traveling parent, with dates, destination, and the child's details, prevents problems at check-in. Some airlines ask for a notarised version; others accept a signed scan. Ask before the trip, not at the gate.
blog6.s4p3: If you expect difficulty getting consent, resolve it well in advance — not the day before travel. Waiting until the last minute turns a paperwork question into a conflict.
blog6.s5title: What the child needs during the switch
blog6.s5p1: Longer summer blocks can be harder on children than the regular weekly switches. They are away from one home for longer, routines change, and friends may be in one place and not the other. The child should know the plan — not every detail, but the shape of the summer. Uncertainty is harder for most children than a plan they do not love.
blog6.s5p2: The child does not need to choose. Framing questions as "do you want to be with mum or dad" puts them in an impossible position. The parents make the plan; the child is told. Let things travel with the child — a favourite stuffed animal, a book, a charger. Small continuity items make longer blocks easier.
blog6.s5p3: Keep transitions calm. The handover day into a two-week block is not the moment for long conversations about rules or expectations. That can wait a day or two.
blog6.s6p1: Summer is often where disagreements surface. One parent wants a trip the other does not approve of. Vacation weeks clash at work. A grandparent visit only works in one specific week. Put the proposal in writing — specific dates, specific plan. It is harder to argue productively in conversation than when both parents can look at the same written version.
blog6.s6p2: Suggest alternatives, not objections alone. "I cannot do week 28, but week 26 or 29 would work" is easier to respond to than "that does not work for me." If direct discussion stalls, family counselling services offer mediation for exactly this — often a few focused sessions on a single practical agreement, like summer.
blog6.s6p3: Once an agreement is reached, write it down. Dates, who has the child when, travel details, handover times and places. A written summary both parents can refer back to prevents re-negotiation in June when somebody remembers a different version.
blog6.ctaDesc: Lina brings together the care schedule, messages, and child information in one shared place — so both parents have the same overview of the summer plan, long before June arrives.

blog7.pageTitle: The first months as co-parents — finding your footing | Lina
blog7.pageDesc: Practical guidance for parents navigating the first months after separation — how to set up routines, communicate clearly, and protect your child through the transition.
blog7.label: Getting started
blog7.title: The first months as co-parents — finding your footing
blog7.intro: The first few months after a separation are often the hardest — practically, emotionally, and logistically. Your child needs predictability, you need to rebuild routines, and the two of you need to find a working rhythm without a clear template. Here is what tends to help in the early weeks, and what to focus on first.
blog7.s1title: Start with the child's everyday life
blog7.s1p1: When everything changes at once, the child benefits most from small, stable anchors. Keep bedtimes, meal times, and school routines as close to normal as possible in both homes.
blog7.s1p2: It is easier to add structure later than to rebuild it after chaos. The first weeks are not the time to introduce big changes — even if something feels overdue.
blog7.s1p3: Protect what already works. If the child has a regular sport, activity, or caregiver, try to keep that constant through the move between homes.
blog7.s2p1: You do not need the final arrangement in week one. A workable temporary schedule — even a rough one — gives the child something to rely on while you figure out what fits long-term.
blog7.s2p2: Many families start with a simple week-on/week-off or every-other-weekend pattern and adjust after a month or two. The point is predictability, not precision.
blog7.s2p3: Write down whatever you agree on, even informally. It prevents the natural drift that happens when everything is spoken but nothing is recorded.
blog7.s3p1: In the early weeks, limit communication to what concerns the child: pickups, school, health, equipment. Everything else can wait.
blog7.s3p2: Short messages are better than long ones. Facts over feelings. Questions over accusations.
blog7.s3p3: Avoid discussing the relationship through the child, and avoid discussing the child through the relationship. Keep the two channels separate from day one.
blog7.s4title: Let the child see both homes function
blog7.s4p1: Children read calm more than they read words. If each home runs smoothly on its own, the child borrows that calm.
blog7.s4p2: You do not need to present a united front on everything. You need to present a stable one each.
blog7.s4p3: Small things matter: a drawer that is theirs, a toothbrush waiting for them, a parent who is ready when they arrive.
blog7.s5title: Make the handovers gentle, not perfect
blog7.s5p1: The first handovers rarely feel natural. That is normal. The goal is not a perfect transition but a predictable one.
blog7.s5p2: Keep goodbyes short, avoid loaded language, and let the other parent take over without commentary. A visible handover to a calm adult is better than a drawn-out farewell.
blog7.s5p3: If the child struggles, do not read it as failure. Transitions take time to settle.
blog7.s6title: Get help before you need it
blog7.s6p1: If something feels stuck — a pattern you cannot break, a conversation you cannot have — a family counsellor or mediator can help early, before it hardens into conflict.
blog7.s6p2: The threshold for reaching out is lower than people think. Early support tends to cost less time, not more.
blog7.s6p3: You do not have to figure this out alone, and the child will not remember the specifics of the first few months — only whether they felt safe.
blog7.ctaDesc: Lina gives both parents a shared place for schedules, equipment, and contacts — so the practical side of the first months takes less effort, and the child has one less thing to carry between homes.

blog8.pageTitle: Writing a care agreement — what it should cover | Lina
blog8.pageDesc: A practical guide to what belongs in a written care agreement between co-parents — schedules, holidays, decisions, and everyday logistics.
blog8.label: Agreement
blog8.title: Writing a care agreement — what it should cover
blog8.intro: A care agreement is not a legal document in most cases — it is a shared understanding put in writing. Its purpose is not to prepare for conflict, but to prevent it. When both parents know what has been agreed, there is less room for drift, misremembering, or quiet resentment. Here is what a calm, workable agreement tends to include.
blog8.s1p1: Start with the week. Who has the child when, where handovers happen, and what time they happen. Concrete beats abstract.
blog8.s1p2: Cover the regular pattern — alternating weeks, 3-4-4-3, 2-2-5-5, or whatever suits your rhythm — and note when it begins.
blog8.s1p3: Include the boundaries of the week. If one parent takes the child on Friday at school pickup, write that down. Ambiguity at the edges causes most of the friction.
blog8.s2p1: School breaks, summer holidays, and public holidays need their own rules — the regular schedule usually pauses during longer breaks.
blog8.s2p2: Agree in advance how holidays are split: alternating years, halving the break, or dividing by named days. Whichever you choose, write it down so future-you does not have to negotiate again.
blog8.s2p3: Include travel. Who informs whom, how much notice is needed, and what consent is required for trips across borders.
blog8.s3p1: Separate everyday decisions from larger ones. Bedtimes, snacks, screen time — each parent decides in their own home. School choice, medical procedures, relocation — both decide together.
blog8.s3p2: Name a default for disagreement. Not a winner, but a process: talk first, bring in a mediator if needed, write down the outcome.
blog8.s3p3: Health information needs to flow freely. Agree that doctor visits, prescriptions, and significant events are shared within a day or two.
blog8.s4p1: An agreement does not need to replace whatever legal child support exists, but it benefits from spelling out what is shared outside of it: activities, equipment, school trips, healthcare costs.
blog8.s4p2: Be concrete about thresholds. "We discuss purchases above a set amount" is easier than "we agree on larger expenses."
blog8.s4p3: Keep a simple record — a shared list or note is enough. Memory is the enemy of fairness here.
blog8.s5p1: Agree on the channel: one app, one shared calendar, one place where practical information lives. Spreading it across texts, emails, and phone calls is how details get lost.
blog8.s5p2: Set expectations for response time. Within 24 hours for non-urgent matters works for most families.
blog8.s5p3: Name what belongs in communication and what does not. The child's needs belong. Past relationship matters do not.
blog8.s6p1: An agreement is a living document. Schedule a review — every six months or once a year — where you read it through together and adjust what no longer fits.
blog8.s6p2: Changes should be written down, not assumed. Verbal updates disappear; written ones survive.
blog8.s6p3: When you disagree, agree on how you disagree. A shared process for working through a stuck point is worth more than perfect drafting.
blog8.ctaTitle: Put the agreement somewhere both of you can see it
blog8.ctaDesc: Lina holds the schedule, shared decisions, and everyday information in one place — so the written agreement stays alive and accessible to both homes.

blog9.pageDesc: How to plan birthdays when a child has two homes — splitting the day, hosting celebrations, and keeping the focus on the child.
blog9.label: Birthdays
blog9.title: Birthdays across two homes
blog9.intro: Birthdays can become one of the most loaded days of the year when a child has two homes. They are meant to be about the child — and yet they often end up reflecting everything unresolved between the parents. A little structure and a lot of restraint can keep the day where it belongs: on the child.
blog9.s1title: Decide early, before the child asks
blog9.s1p1: Children start thinking about their birthday weeks in advance. If the two parents have not agreed on how the day will go, the child ends up as the messenger. Decide before that happens.
blog9.s1p2: Revisit the plan each year. Young children want both parents in the same place; older children often prefer two smaller celebrations; teenagers usually have opinions of their own.
blog9.s1p3: Put the decision in writing, even briefly. "This year: cake at one home on Saturday, party at the other on Sunday" is enough.
blog9.s2p1: There is no right answer. Two celebrations can feel generous — or it can feel like performing the same day twice. One shared celebration can feel inclusive — or it can feel forced.
blog9.s2p2: Read the child. For younger children, one calm celebration with both parents present is often easier. For older children, two separate ones can give them space to enjoy each without tension.
blog9.s2p3: Whatever you pick, keep it proportional. Two parties should not turn into a competition.
blog9.s3p1: If the birthday falls on a school day, the easiest pattern is that the child wakes up with one parent and has dinner or a cake with the other. Morning and evening, not a tug-of-war in between.
blog9.s3p2: If the birthday falls on a weekend or holiday, consider splitting the day into two halves — each parent gets a clear block rather than a shifting one.
blog9.s3p3: Handovers on a birthday should be especially gentle. No handoff of frustration — just the child, their things, and a calm exchange.
blog9.s4p1: Gifts become a proxy for many other things when parents are not careful. Agree on a general range, or at least on what the big gift is and which parent is giving it.
blog9.s4p2: Avoid duplicating significant presents. A child does not need two bikes, and the second one always feels like second place.
blog9.s4p3: If extended family wants to give, coordinate loosely so the child does not receive five similar things. A shared list works well for this.
blog9.s5p1: If you are hosting a friends' party, one parent usually takes the lead — but the other should be informed of the date, guest list, and logistics. Surprise is not a feature here.
blog9.s5p2: Consider whether both parents attend. Younger children usually benefit from both being present for the main celebration, if the atmosphere stays calm. Older children sometimes prefer only one.
blog9.s5p3: If only one parent attends, keep the other updated. A photo or a short message after means the absent parent is not last to know about their child's day.
blog9.s6title: When the child is at the other home
blog9.s6p1: If the birthday falls in the other parent's week, resist the urge to reclaim part of the day. The calendar is not everything. A call in the morning, a card, or a short visit often matters more than equal hours.
blog9.s6p2: Send gifts across in advance, not at the last minute. The child should not be handed something that arrived in a rush.
blog9.s6p3: Trust the other parent to make the day good. Your role, on this particular day, may be the quiet one — and the child will notice that too.
blog9.ctaTitle: A calmer birthday, from both homes
blog9.ctaDesc: Lina keeps birthdays, contacts, and shared plans in one place — so both parents can prepare the day without last-minute coordination, and the child keeps the focus.
